name: Commit-Required Harness Doctrine Installer
sprintRole: Install universal harness discipline as tracked repository doctrine
useWhen: Baseline harness doctrine must be installed or repaired in the repository before serious work.
inspectFirst: Current repo rules, AGENTS.md or equivalent instruction surfaces, validators, tests, manifests, branch state, and PR conventions.
expectedOutput: Repo-native doctrine and an enforcement test or validator committed to the relevant repository, with push or PR evidence.
nextStep: Use the task-specific prompt only after the doctrine commit and PR update are proven.
proofGate: Changed tracked doctrine files, enforcement validation, commit SHA, branch state, push confirmation, and opened or updated PR evidence.
copyContent:
INSTALL THE HARNESS DOCTRINE NOW. DO NOT MERELY ACKNOWLEDGE, SUMMARIZE, OR REPEAT THESE RULES.

Repo: resolve the current repository from the environment when no explicit repo is supplied.
Branch or worktree: preserve existing work; use the current safe branch or create an isolated feature branch when needed.
Sprint: Commit-Required Harness Doctrine Installation.
Lane: repository doctrine and enforcement.
Owned scope: repo-native agent rules, doctrine references, focused validators or tests, and the PR metadata needed to prove installation.
Forbidden scope: unrelated product changes, destructive cleanup, force-push, default-branch mutation, secrets, private evidence, or generated runtime junk.
Expected artifacts: tracked doctrine, an enforcement test or validator, validation output, commit SHA, push confirmation, and an opened or updated PR.
Validation order: targeted doctrine contract, relevant instruction or harness validators, broader safe checks, then final Git review.

START WITH BOUNDED PREFLIGHT
Run:
git status --short
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline --decorate -5
If the worktree is dirty and this sprint does not own those changes, preserve them and use an isolated worktree or branch.

INSPECT BEFORE INVENTING
Inspect existing AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, repository instruction files, harness doctrine, codebase maps, workflow registries, validators, tests, hooks, generated-output policy, and branch or PR conventions.
Reuse the repository's established authority surface. Do not create a second competing doctrine file when a canonical one already exists.

REQUIRED REPOSITORY ACTION
Modify tracked repository files so the doctrine is actually installed, not merely active in this conversation.
At minimum:
- write or repair the canonical repo agent-rule or doctrine surface
- preserve task-specific instructions as higher-priority execution contracts
- add or update a focused test, validator, hook, or manifest rule that rejects acknowledgment-only setup
- connect the doctrine to the repository's existing harness or documentation index when that pattern exists

The installed doctrine must require agents to name:
- repo
- branch or worktree
- PR or sprint
- lane
- owned scope
- forbidden scope
- expected artifacts
- validation order when specified

It must enforce this executable loop:
request -> evidence review -> bounded decision -> repo or Git or GitHub mutation -> artifacts -> validation -> report -> next decision

It must also enforce:
- evidence before confidence
- preservation before destructive cleanup
- existing contracts and helpers before invention
- no completion claim without checks, artifacts, commit or GitHub evidence
- no substitution of a rewritten prompt, plan, or handoff for requested repository work
- task-specific execution rules override generic closeout behavior

ACTION-COMMITMENT RULE
Any prompt that claims it will install, set up, build, execute, repair, configure, upgrade, deploy, merge, or release something must require the corresponding mutation and proof.
A title or expected output that claims action while the prompt permits acknowledgment, advice, or a plan is invalid.

VALIDATE AND COMMIT
Run the strongest practical focused checks and any repository-owned instruction or harness validators.
Then run:
git diff --check
git status --short
git diff --stat
git diff
Stage only owned tracked files.
Run git commit -m with a useful doctrine-installation message.
Run git push -u origin for the safe feature branch.
Open or update a pull request and record the exact commit SHA and validation evidence.

ALREADY-COMPLETE PATH
Do not create meaningless churn when the exact doctrine and enforcement already exist.
Instead, prove the tracked files and passing checks, then make an owned GitHub mutation such as correcting the PR body or recording the validation evidence.

BLOCKER PATH
When repository mutation is genuinely impossible, stop with the exact blocker, the smallest patch or file content that should be applied, and one safest next command.

INVALID RESPONSES
- acknowledgment only
- summary only
- rewritten prompt only
- plan only
- handoff only
- preflight only
- a request for permission when the bounded mutation is safe

FINAL REPORT
Report repo, branch or worktree, PR or sprint, lane, owned and forbidden scope, files changed, validation commands and results, commit SHA, push or PR mutation, gaps, final Git state, and one exact next command.

name: Commit-Required Repo-Local Harness Builder
sprintRole: Build and complete a repo-local agent harness
useWhen: A repository lacks, fragments, or has stale agent rules, maps, workflows, registries, validators, scoped skills, hooks, or operator reports.
inspectFirst: Existing repo rules, docs, scripts, tests, validators, manifests, hooks, generated-output policy, branch and PR conventions.
expectedOutput: Implemented and validated repo-local harness contributions committed to the relevant repository.
nextStep: Run P11 or P12 after the harness commit and PR update.
proofGate: Changed tracked files, validators pass, commit exists, and push or PR state is reported.
copyContent:
Every serious app should develop a repo-local AI harness.
Do not treat prompts as the harness. Prompts are one artifact inside it.
The harness should include:
- repo agent rules
- codebase map
- workflow specs
- run context
- artifact registry
- validators
- local hooks where useful
- scoped skills
- read-only code intelligence where useful
- English/operator reports
- final handoff compression
Before inventing, inspect existing repo rules, test runners, validators, docs, scripts, manifests, generated-output policy, and branch/PR conventions.
Goal: complete the harness, validate the owned changes, commit and push the coherent contributions, and report the resulting Git or PR evidence; do not stop at a plan, description, prompt pack, or handoff.
A fresh agent should enter the repo, inspect the harness, choose the right workflow, run the right validators, avoid known traps, produce artifacts, and hand off cleanly.

name: Conversation Closeout + Executable Harness-Build Sprint Mapper
sprintRole: Convert chat into implementation launch order and assign harness/application builders
useWhen: A chat contains decisions, unfinished work, or harness gaps that must become self-contained implementation sprints.
inspectFirst: Current conversation and known repo context.
expectedOutput: Launch order, executable build panels with named harness/application owners, and a supporting sprint map.
nextStep: Launch the first implementation panel; do not stop at the map.
proofGate: Every identified harness gap has an executable build owner or cited evidence that no build is needed.
copyContent:
We covered a lot and only progressed partway. Convert this chat into a sprint map and executable launch pack.
Preserve only context that prevents rework. Do not summarize every topic equally.
SOURCE OF TRUTH
Use the best available chat context and, when repo access exists, verify current repo, branch, PR, worktree, test, validator, artifact, and CI state.
Prefer repository evidence over remembered chat context. Mark stale or uncertain claims explicitly.
OUTPUT ORDER — LAUNCH ORDER FIRST
Analyze the material before writing, but the first substantive section must be LAUNCH ORDER.
1. LAUNCH ORDER
Put the launch order at the very top immediately after the title.
Name the first chat, every dependency gate, each parallel group, and the final convergence chat.
Number and title the prompts exactly as they will appear below.
The prompt panels must be displayed in the exact same order as this launch order.
Do not describe one launch order and present the panels in another order.
Do not include an alternate display order, category order, or convenience order.
Keep prompts in the same parallel group contiguous under that group.
State: one prompt panel goes into one new chat.
2. COMPACT COORDINATION PREAMBLE
Place one short readable preamble after the launch order and before the panels.
Include:
- mission
- repo and primary local path if known
- current branch, PR, and verified floor if known
- wave order and hard dependencies
- safe parallel lanes
- principal collision risks
- proof ceiling and critical safety boundary
The preamble is for orientation only.
Do not output a shared chat preamble that must be copied separately.
Every prompt panel must contain all context required to run that chat by itself.
3. NEXT CHAT PANELS
Immediately after the preamble, emit every next-chat prompt in its own separate writing block or copyable panel.
Each panel must have its own copy control.
Display panels in the exact order already stated in LAUNCH ORDER.
Do not put all prompts in one code fence.
Do not put two prompts in one panel.
Do not place commentary, analysis, or summaries between panels.
Do not require the operator to combine text from multiple sections.
Each panel must be self-contained and executable.
Every panel must include:
- banner and sprint name
- repo, local path if known, branch, PR, wave, and lane
- dependencies and safe parallel work
- owned scope and forbidden scope
- expected artifacts and mission
- read-first files and compact preflight
- numbered tasks
- sprint-specific safety
- validation order
- commit, push, and PR contract when repo mutation is intended
- proof level, evidence, and proof ceiling
- exact final response contract
- exact next command
- an explicit instruction to BUILD, REPAIR, VALIDATE, or RESEARCH the owned surface
- when repo mutation is intended: inspect, implement, validate, commit, push, and open or update the PR
- never leave required harness construction as an unassigned description
Use a short title immediately above each panel.
4. HARNESS AND APPLICATION FACTORING PASS
Before assigning sprint ownership, classify every lingering requirement into one primary surface:
- harness spine: run context, artifact registry, schemas, validators, fixtures, workflows, proof gates
- agent harness: skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, instruction surfaces, manifests, MCP/tools, hooks, context injection
- conventional application logic: domain models, services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, deployment orchestration
- integration seam: interfaces between harness, agent routing, and application logic
- docs/reporting
- validation
- runtime proof
- research/design
For every skill, capability, or trigger, decide: keep, create, split, merge, retire, or rewire.
Record for each:
- owner
- deterministic activation condition
- inputs and outputs
- preconditions and forbidden conditions
- guardrails
- tests and proof level
- files or manifests likely to change
Use these boundaries:
- skills describe reusable workflow guidance
- capabilities expose reusable operations
- triggers deterministically route conditions to a skill, capability, or workflow
- application logic remains in code and domain contracts, not hidden in prompts
- prompts may orchestrate work but must not become the only implementation of product behavior
Create separate factoring sprints when agent harness work and application logic would otherwise collide.
5. HARNESS BUILD OWNERSHIP
Do not stop at describing, classifying, or mapping the harness.
When the factoring pass finds missing, partial, stale, or unvalidated harness spine or agent-harness work, the launch order MUST include one or more executable build sprints that direct AI agents to implement it in the repository.
Every required harness surface must have an owner panel or explicit repository evidence that the current implementation is sufficient.
A harness-build panel must instruct the assigned agent to:
- inspect existing repo rules, harness files, validators, workflows, manifests, hooks, and generated-output policy before inventing
- create or repair the owned harness files, schemas, fixtures, skills, capabilities, triggers, workflows, hooks, and integration seams
- keep application behavior in code and domain contracts rather than prompts
- run the exact harness and repository validators
- commit coherent changes
- push normally and open or update the intended PR when the environment allows
- report exact artifacts, validation evidence, commit SHA, PR state, proof level, proof ceiling, and next command
Separate harness-spine, agent-harness, and application-logic build sprints when they would collide in the same files or ownership lane.
Research-only panels may precede implementation only when a real unknown blocks safe construction; they must name the exact downstream build panel they unblock.
If no harness build is required, state the inspected files and evidence proving the existing harness already satisfies the requirement.
The output is incomplete when it identifies a harness gap but assigns no AI agent to build or repair it.
6. SUPPORTING SPRINT MAP
Place this section after all copyable panels.
Include:
MISSION
- what we are trying to accomplish
- why it matters
- what success looks like
TOPICS COVERED
For each topic include status, useful context, and likely repo impact.
Use statuses: decided, partial, unresolved, rejected, or future idea.
DECISIONS MADE
- product decisions
- technical decisions
- naming, contracts, and patterns
- things explicitly rejected
CURRENT STATE
- repo or project
- branch, PR, or sprint
- recent changes
- stale or uncertain context
FACTORING MAP
- harness spine
- skills
- capabilities
- triggers and routing
- conventional application logic
- integration seams
- shared contracts and collision ownership
LINGERING WORK
Classify remaining work into feature, harness, agent-harness factoring, application-logic factoring, cleanup, validation, docs/reporting, runtime proof, research/design, and parallel-safe sprints.
TARGETS TO INSPECT FIRST
Name exact files, directories, scripts, docs, logs, commands, manifests, skills, capabilities, triggers, schemas, workflows, or artifacts.
RISKS AND GAPS
Name unknowns, fragile assumptions, blockers, collision risks, proof gaps, and forbidden scope.
7. QUALITY RULES
Make panels bold, direct, compact, and execution-oriented.
Avoid permission theater, duplicate ownership, trivial-only progress, and giant monolithic prompts.
Do not claim a higher proof level from a lower one.
Do not invent repo facts when evidence is unavailable.
Do not repeat a second launch order after the panels.

name: Repository Evidence + First Sprint Executor
sprintRole: Analyze the repo and execute the first safe sprint
useWhen: Entering an unfamiliar, stale, or context-heavy repo.
inspectFirst: Git floor, rules, architecture, manifests, PRs, plans, tests, validators, artifacts.
expectedOutput: Cited evidence pack, ranked queue, and first bounded repo mutation.
nextStep: Use P11 or P12.
proofGate: Path-grounded evidence plus actual repo movement.
copyContent:
ANALYZE THE REPOSITORY, BUILD THE EVIDENCE PACK, THEN EXECUTE THE FIRST SAFE SPRINT. DO NOT STOP AT A PLAN.
Repo: xyz_repo_or_path
Branch / PR if known: xyz_branch
Context or plan path if available: xyz_plan_directory
MISSION
Recover operational truth from the repository itself, derive a ranked sprint queue from evidence, and immediately execute the highest-value bounded sprint that is safe in the current environment.
Treat the current repository as the source of truth over remembered chat context.
Collect all actionable evidence, not every byte or generated dependency.
DO NOT ANSWER WITH
- a repo summary only
- a plan only
- a prompt pack only
- generic best practices without file-path evidence
- a request for permission when a safe bounded sprint is available
- confidence based only on filenames, timestamps, or stale handoff prose
COMPACT PREFLIGHT
Run what is available and record exact skips:
git rev-parse --show-toplevel
git status --short
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline --decorate -12
git worktree list
git remote -v
gh pr list --state open --limit 30
gh pr status
If the worktree is dirty or conflicted:
- preserve existing work
- identify the owning branch, files, and likely sprint
- use or create an isolated worktree when this lane does not own the dirt
- make repo-floor repair the first sprint only when it is the safest useful move
- otherwise stop with the exact blocker and safest next command
REPOSITORY EVIDENCE HARVEST
Inspect the repository before selecting work:
- local operating law: AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .ai/, skills, workflow rules, CONTRIBUTING, README
- architecture and entry points: source roots, services, launchers, apps, packages, modules
- manifests and toolchain: build files, dependency files, task runners, lockfiles, environment contracts
- recent center of gravity: recent commits, changed files, active branches, open PRs, review comments when available
- plans and state: docs, status files, roadmaps, handoffs, decision logs, release notes
- validation surface: tests, validators, linters, type checks, builds, CI workflows, fixtures, smoke scripts
- harness spine: run context, artifact registry, schemas, workflow specs, reports, hooks, codebase maps
- agent leverage: repo-local prompts, scoped skills, MCP/LSP or read-only navigation helpers
- artifacts and output policy: generated files, logs, reports, ignored paths, committed evidence, cleanup rules
- unresolved signals: TODO, FIXME, XXX, NotImplemented, skipped tests, quarantines, stubs, failing checks
- runtime and deployment seams: launch scripts, installers, browser flows, target mutation boundaries, safety docs
- stale or conflicting context: placeholder plans, superseded docs, abandoned branches, contradictory instructions
EVIDENCE RULES
- cite exact file paths, commands, commit SHAs, PR numbers, and validator names
- distinguish FACT, INFERENCE, and UNKNOWN
- use document content and git history to judge freshness; timestamps alone are weak evidence
- search existing contracts, helpers, names, scripts, and output patterns before inventing
- do not expose secrets, credentials, personal data, or huge generated logs
- do not crawl vendored dependencies or build outputs unless they are directly relevant
- name unavailable tools and the exact command that should be run later
BUILD THE REPO EVIDENCE PACK
Produce a compact operational ledger with these sections:
1. REPO IDENTITY
- repo path, branch, HEAD, remotes, worktrees, dirty/conflicted state, open PRs
2. CURRENT CENTER OF GRAVITY
- what recent commits and changed files show the repo is actively becoming
3. ACTIVE WORKSTREAMS
- feature, harness, cleanup, validation, docs/reporting, runtime proof, release/PR hygiene, research/design
4. HARNESS INVENTORY
- present, partial, missing, duplicated, or stale: rules, map, workflows, run context, registry, validators, hooks, skills, reports
5. PRODUCT / FEATURE INVENTORY
- implemented surfaces, incomplete surfaces, entry points, and integration boundaries
6. VALIDATION AND PROOF SURFACE
- commands, CI jobs, fixtures, artifacts, and the highest proof each can support
7. UNRESOLVED SIGNALS
- TODOs, failing or skipped checks, placeholders, stale plans, review requests, unmerged useful work
8. RISKS AND FORBIDDEN SCOPE
- mutation risks, credentials, live targets, destructive operations, unrelated rewrite zones
9. IMPORTANT PATHS
- the small set of files and directories the next action depends on
DERIVE THE SPRINT QUEUE
Create a ranked list of bounded sprint candidates.
For each candidate include:
- title and lane
- evidence and why now
- owned scope and forbidden scope
- dependencies and collision risks
- files to inspect and files likely to change
- expected artifacts
- validation commands
- risk and proof ceiling
- whether it is safe in parallel
FIRST-SPRINT SELECTION RULES
- floor before furniture: repair unsafe repo state before feature work
- shared contracts before reports, dashboards, MCP consumers, or duplicated output shapes
- unblock the highest number of later sprints with the smallest bounded change
- prefer a real tested slice over broad documentation or speculative refactoring
- do not select live mutation or runtime proof when the environment or scope forbids it
EXECUTE THE FIRST SAFE SPRINT
State repo, branch, PR/sprint, lane, owned scope, forbidden scope, and expected artifacts.
Then immediately do one of these:
1. Modify useful tracked repo files within the selected sprint.
2. Prove the selected sprint is already complete with committed files and passing checks.
3. Stop with an exact blocker plus the smallest patch, diff, or file content that should be applied.
Default to option 1.
Work rules:
- reuse repository patterns
- keep changes bounded
- avoid unrelated rewrites
- add or update tests, validators, scripts, manifests, or operational docs that enforce the behavior
- do not create a tracked census document merely to narrate the scan
- persist a repo-intake artifact only when repo conventions or downstream workflows need it
- keep generated evidence in approved ignored paths unless sanitized tracked evidence is explicitly required
VALIDATE AND DELIVER
Run targeted tests, relevant validators/static checks, build checks, and broader checks when practical.
Run git diff --check, git status --short, git diff --stat, and review the final diff.
Commit and push or open/update a PR when the environment allows.
Do not claim a higher proof level than the evidence supports.
FINAL RESPONSE FORMAT
REPO EVIDENCE
- identity, center of gravity, active workstreams, harness gaps, important paths
SPRINT QUEUE
- ranked candidates and the first-sprint decision
WORK COMMITTED
- summary, files changed, artifacts, commit SHA, push/PR state
VALIDATION
- commands, results, skipped checks, proof level reached
GAPS / RISKS
- blockers, unknowns, forbidden scope, collision risks
FINAL GIT STATE
- git status --short
NEXT COMMAND
- one exact command
Do not include a next-agent prompt. Use P12 only after the repository-derived sprint is complete.

name: [PARALLEL] Repo-Aware Sprint + Harness Factoring Distributor
sprintRole: Divide remaining work into bounded lanes
useWhen: Repo state is known but harness, agent, and application work need explicit factoring and ownership.
inspectFirst: P03 evidence or current repo state.
expectedOutput: Launch order first, ordered copy-panel sprint candidates, plus harness, skill, capability, trigger, and app-logic factoring ledgers.
nextStep: Use P05 or P07.
proofGate: Dependencies, collision ownership, and exact panel sequence are explicit.
copyContent:
You are entering an existing repository with prior chat context and need to factor the remaining work into bounded sprints.
Repo:
`xyz_repo_or_path`
Plan or context path if available:
`xyz_plan_directory`
If the plan path is missing, stale, blank, or placeholder, do not stall.
Inspect the repository, current branch, recent commits, open PRs, worktrees, status docs, handoffs, plans, tests, validators, workflows, artifacts, skills, capabilities, triggers, and current git state.
Prefer current repository evidence over remembered context.
COMPACT PREFLIGHT
Record:
- repo
- primary local path if known
- branch
- head
- status
- worktrees
- open PRs
- recent center of gravity
- likely active sprint
- stale or missing context
Inspect before distributing work:
- recent commits and changed files
- open PR diffs, checks, reviews, and dependencies
- docs, plans, handoffs, and codebase maps
- schemas, run context, artifact registry, validators, and fixtures
- workflows, launchers, and runtime evidence
- agent skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, manifests, MCP/tools, and hooks
- application services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, and deployment logic
FACTORING PASS
Classify each topic into one primary ownership surface:
- floor or cleanup
- harness spine
- agent harness
- conventional application logic
- integration seam
- validation
- docs/reporting
- runtime proof
- research/design
- unsafe or blocked
For agent-harness surfaces, inventory every relevant skill, capability, and trigger.
For each, determine whether to keep, create, split, merge, retire, or rewire it.
Require deterministic activation conditions, explicit inputs and outputs, preconditions, guardrails, owner, tests, and proof ceiling.
Use these boundaries:
- skills are reusable workflow guidance
- capabilities are reusable operations or tool-backed actions
- triggers route deterministic conditions to a skill, capability, or workflow
- product and domain behavior belongs in application code and contracts
- prompts must not become the only implementation of application logic
Separate harness factoring from application-logic factoring when they own different files or proof gates.
Detect shared-file, shared-schema, shared-workflow, skill, capability, trigger, and PR-stacking collisions before recommending parallel work.
OUTPUT ORDER — LAUNCH ORDER FIRST
Analyze fully, then output in this order:
1. LAUNCH ORDER
Put the launch order at the very top.
Name the first chat, proof gate, parallel groups, waiting lanes, and final convergence.
Number and title the panels exactly as they will appear.
Display the panels below in that exact order.
Never use a different display order, category order, or convenience order.
Keep every parallel group contiguous.
State: one prompt panel goes into one new chat.
2. COMPACT COORDINATION PREAMBLE
Include repo floor, waves, hard dependencies, safe parallel lanes, collision owners, safety boundary, and proof ceiling.
The preamble is not a separately copied prompt fragment.
Do not output a shared preamble that the operator must paste above each sprint.
3. NEXT-CHAT SPRINT PANELS
Emit each complete next-chat prompt in its own separate writing block or copyable panel.
Each panel must have its own copy control.
Panels must appear in the exact launch order listed above.
Do not place all prompts in one code fence.
Do not place two prompts in one panel.
Do not make the operator copy global rules from another section.
Each panel must be self-contained and include:
- banner and sprint name
- repo, local path if known, branch, PR, wave, and lane
- hard dependencies and safe parallel work
- owned scope and forbidden scope
- expected artifacts
- mission
- read-first files
- compact preflight
- numbered tasks
- safety
- validation order
- commit, push, and PR contract when mutation is intended
- proof level and proof ceiling
- exact final response contract
- exact next command
Put only a short sprint title above each panel and no commentary between panels.
4. SUPPORTING FACTORING LEDGER
After the panels, provide:
TOPICS FOUND
Classify feature, harness, agent harness, application logic, integration, cleanup, validation, docs/reporting, runtime proof, release/PR hygiene, research/design, and blocked topics.
HARNESS FACTORING
List run context, artifact registry, schemas, validators, fixtures, workflows, skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, instruction surfaces, manifests, MCP/tools, hooks, and ownership.
APPLICATION LOGIC FACTORING
List domain services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, jobs, deployment orchestration, and ownership.
SPRINT CANDIDATES
For each include title, goal, why now, owned scope, forbidden scope, files to inspect, files likely to change, expected outputs, validation, dependencies, parallel safety, risk level, and proof ceiling.
RECOMMENDED EXECUTION ORDER
Repeat only a compact confirmation that the panel sequence matched the launch order.
Do not introduce a new or different order here.
Put floor and shared contracts before reports, dashboards, MCP, agents, or UI consumers.
QUALITY RULES
Be ambitious but practical.
Avoid permission theater, duplicate ownership, giant prompts, and trivial-only progress.
Do not claim higher proof from lower proof.

name: [SERIALIZED] Ordered Sprint Plan Pack Generator
sprintRole: Generate a multi-chat launch pack
useWhen: Multiple agents or sequential chats need one-click launch panels, bounded factoring, and proof rules.
inspectFirst: Repo evidence, candidate lanes, branch/PR state.
expectedOutput: Launch order at the top, compact coordination preamble, then one self-contained copy panel per sprint in that exact order.
nextStep: Launch P06/P07/P08 as required.
proofGate: Every lane is independently copyable; panel display equals launch order; no shared-preamble assembly.
copyContent:
Create a sprint plan pack for parallel or sequential AI chats.
OPERATING RULE
One sprint panel goes into one new chat.
Do not paste multiple sprint panels into the same chat.
Build the pack from the best available context in this chat and, when repo access exists, from current repository evidence.
Prefer current repo, branch, PR, worktree, test, validator, workflow, artifact, skill, capability, trigger, and CI state over remembered context.
OUTPUT PRESENTATION CONTRACT
The first substantive section must be LAUNCH ORDER.
Do not begin with a shared copyable preamble.
Do not require the operator to assemble a prompt from multiple boxes.
Every sprint panel must be self-contained.
1. LAUNCH ORDER — PUT THIS AT THE TOP
State the first chat to launch, why it is first, the exact proof gate that unlocks later work, every parallel group, waiting lanes, and final convergence.
Number and title every sprint exactly as it will appear below.
The sprint panels must be displayed in the exact same order as this launch order.
Do not present panels in category order, numeric convenience order, or any order different from the recommended launch order.
Do not offer an alternate display order.
Keep prompts in each parallel group contiguous and label the group once.
Do not repeat a conflicting launch order at the bottom.
2. COMPACT COORDINATION PREAMBLE
After the launch order, provide one short readable preamble in normal markdown.
Include:
- repo
- primary local path if known
- current branch, main SHA, active PR, or verified floor when known
- first commands to inspect state
- dirty or conflicted worktree rule
- worktree policy
- proof requirements
- what must not be committed
- safety constraints
- branch and PR expectations
- wave order
- hard dependencies
- safe parallel lanes
- collision risks and ownership
Keep this preamble compact enough to scan without repeated scrolling.
The preamble is orientation only and is not copied separately into sprint chats.
Every panel below repeats the essential rules it needs.
3. FACTORING PASS
Before creating sprint panels, factor the work into these ownership surfaces:
- floor and cleanup
- harness spine
- agent harness
- conventional application logic
- integration seams
- validation
- docs/reporting
- runtime proof
- research/design
For the harness spine, identify run context, artifact registry, schemas, validators, fixtures, workflows, proof gates, and shared output contracts.
For the agent harness, identify skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, instruction surfaces, manifests, MCP/tools, hooks, and context injection.
For conventional application logic, identify domain models, services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, jobs, and deployment orchestration.
For every skill, capability, or trigger, decide whether to keep, create, split, merge, retire, or rewire it.
Record:
- canonical owner
- deterministic activation condition
- inputs and outputs
- preconditions and forbidden conditions
- guardrails
- tests
- proof level
- files and manifests likely to change
Use these boundaries:
- skills describe reusable workflow guidance
- capabilities expose reusable operations or tool-backed actions
- triggers deterministically route conditions to a skill, capability, or workflow
- application and domain behavior remains in code and contracts
- prompts may orchestrate work but must not become the only implementation of product logic
Create separate factoring sprints when agent harness work and application logic own different files, contracts, or proof gates.
4. PROOF TAXONOMY
Define only the proof levels relevant to the application, including when applicable:
- contract proof
- harness proof
- static test proof
- build proof
- launcher or browser proof
- command ACK proof
- movement or behavior observed proof
- live runtime proof
- deployment completion proof
- operator acceptance proof
State clearly: do not claim a higher proof level from a lower one.
Include concrete non-equivalences such as unit PASS not equaling launcher proof, command ACK not equaling observed behavior, and fixture E2E not equaling live runtime proof.
5. WAVES, DEPENDENCIES, AND COLLISIONS
Group work into:
- Wave 0: clean or prove the floor
- Wave A: harness spine
- Wave B: conventional application and product reliability
- Wave C: agent harness leverage
- Wave D: integration and cleanup
Use the floor-before-furniture rule:
Reports, dashboards, catalogs, MCP, skills, agents, and UI layers may not invent output shapes before canonical run context and artifact registry contracts exist.
Name which sprint establishes the floor and which later sprints must wait.
Name:
- hard dependencies
- soft dependencies
- safe parallel lanes
- shared-file collision risks
- shared-schema collision risks
- shared-workflow collision risks
- skill, capability, and trigger ownership collisions
- branch and PR stacking risks
- the single owner for each shared surface
Do not assign the same shared file, schema, workflow, skill, capability, or trigger to multiple simultaneous sprint owners.
6. SPRINT PANELS
Immediately after the preamble, output every sprint as its own separate writing block or copyable panel.
Each panel must have its own copy control.
Order the panels in the exact launch order stated at the top.
Do not put all sprint prompts in one giant code fence.
Do not put two sprint prompts in one panel.
Do not place long analysis between sprint panels.
Do not require the operator to combine text from multiple sections.
Put only a short sprint title immediately above each panel.
Every panel must contain the complete prompt to paste into one new AI chat.
Every sprint panel must include:
BANNER
SPRINT NAME
REPO
PRIMARY LOCAL PATH
BRANCH
PR, IF KNOWN
WAVE
LANE
DEPENDENCIES
SAFE PARALLEL WORK
OWNED SCOPE
FORBIDDEN SCOPE
EXPECTED ARTIFACTS
MISSION
READ FIRST
COMPACT PREFLIGHT
DIRTY WORKTREE RULE
TASKS
SAFETY
VALIDATION
COMMIT AND PUSH CONTRACT
PROOF CONTRACT
FINAL RESPONSE CONTRACT
EXACT NEXT COMMAND
COMPACT PREFLIGHT must include at minimum:
git rev-parse --show-toplevel
git status --short
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline --decorate -12
git worktree list
git remote -v
git fetch --all --prune
gh pr list --state open --limit 50
gh pr status
Add exact PR inspection commands when relevant.
DIRTY WORKTREE RULE must require preserving unknown work, avoiding destructive cleanup, and using an isolated worktree when the sprint does not own the dirt.
TASKS must be numbered and name exact files, contracts, scripts, validators, tests, skills, capabilities, triggers, workflows, and artifacts when evidence supports them.
VALIDATION must list targeted tests, validators, static checks, build or launcher checks, broader regressions, git diff and status checks, and final CI in execution order.
COMMIT AND PUSH CONTRACT must require:
git diff --check
git status --short
git diff --stat
git diff
git add <changed tracked files>
git commit -m "<useful message>"
git push -u origin <branch>
Require opening or updating the correct PR when appropriate.
PROOF CONTRACT must require proof level reached, supporting evidence, proof ceiling, and missing live or operator evidence.
FINAL RESPONSE CONTRACT must require:
CONTEXT
COMPLETED WORK
VALIDATION
GENERATED ARTIFACTS
KNOWN GAPS / RISKS
SKIPPED CHECKS
FINAL GIT STATE
NEXT COMMAND
NEXT-AGENT HANDOFF
Require changed files, commits, PR URL, validation output, artifact paths, git status, skipped checks with exact later commands, and the next bounded owner.
7. EXECUTION REQUIREMENT
Every sprint panel must tell the new chat to execute repository work and not stop at a plan.
Require at least one useful repository or GitHub mutation unless the sprint is already proven complete or an exact blocker prevents safe mutation.
A valid result must include changed tracked files, a commit SHA, push or PR mutation, branch or worktree mutation, merge or cherry-pick evidence, or an exact blocker with the smallest useful patch or diff.
8. SUPPORTING LEDGER
After all panels, include the factoring ledger, dependency map, collision ledger, proof taxonomy, and supporting rationale.
This ledger may explain the launch order but may not change it.
Do not add a second alternative order.
FINAL QUALITY RULES
Make the pack practical, bold, compact, and executable.
Optimize for one-click copying, minimal scrolling, self-contained prompts, explicit ownership, and clean agent handoff.
Do not optimize only for reading.
Do not output one monolithic markdown document containing all sprint prompts in one code fence.
Do not hide operational instructions outside the sprint panels.
Do not allow skills, capabilities, triggers, or application logic to remain unfactored when they create separate ownership or proof requirements.

name: Repo / PR Cleanup Executor
sprintRole: Clean repo and PR state with preservation-first authority
useWhen: The user wants cherry-picks, repairs, merges, comments, branch mutations, and PR closure.
inspectFirst: Branches, worktrees, open PRs, unique commits, checks, dependencies.
expectedOutput: Preserved useful work, repaired/merged/closed PRs, clean base.
nextStep: Use P07 for remaining feature work or P12 to close.
proofGate: Actual Git/GitHub mutations and disposition proof.
copyContent:
CLEAN THE REPOSITORY AND PR FLOOR. CHERRY-PICK FIRST; REPAIR, MERGE, COMMENT, AND CLOSE. DO NOT STOP AT ANALYSIS.
Repo: xyz_repo_or_path
Sprint: xyz_sprint_name
Owned scope: xyz_owned_scope
Forbidden scope: xyz_forbidden_scope
Context or plan path: xyz_plan_directory
Validation order: xyz_validation_order
MISSION
Leave the repository, branches, worktrees, and pull requests cleaner than you found them.
You have full authority inside owned scope to preserve useful work, create safe branches or worktrees, cherry-pick commits, repair PR branches, commit, push, update PR metadata, comment, label, retarget, mark draft or ready, merge, and close superseded PRs.
The default posture is preservation-first, not deletion-first.
Cherry-pick unique useful work before closing or abandoning its source PR.
Respect explicit validation order. When the user says to save Pester, survey, integration, or other expensive checks for last, do that.
DO NOT ANSWER WITH
- a repo or PR inventory only
- a cleanup plan only
- a list of commands the user should run
- a request for permission when authority and scope are already explicit
- a destructive cleanup proposal that has not preserved unique work
- a handoff instead of repository or PR mutations
COMPACT PREFLIGHT
Run what is available:
git status --short
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline --decorate -8
git worktree list
git fetch --all --prune
gh pr list --state open --limit 50
gh pr status
If the primary worktree is dirty, conflicted, stale, or owned by another lane:
- preserve it
- use an existing safe worktree or create an isolated cleanup worktree
- never discard uncommitted work merely to obtain a clean base
PR DISPOSITION MAP
Classify every in-scope PR or branch:
- merge-ready
- repairable in place
- unique commits to cherry-pick
- superseded after preservation
- duplicate with no unique value
- blocked by CI, review, credentials, or base divergence
- intentionally deferred until later validation
PRESERVATION-FIRST RULES
- inspect commit and file deltas before deciding
- preserve unique commits on an integration or destination branch
- prefer cherry-pick, revert, restore, or bounded branch repair over deletion
- merge clean green predecessors in dependency order
- close a PR only after its useful work is merged, cherry-picked, replaced, or proven unnecessary
- leave a concise closing comment naming where useful work went
- do not delete remote branches unless explicitly requested or clearly allowed by repo policy after preservation proof
- do not force-push unless explicitly authorized and no safer repair exists
EXECUTION
Perform useful repo or PR mutations immediately.
Valid movement includes:
- creating a branch or worktree
- cherry-picking or reverting commits
- restoring unrelated file drift
- resolving conflicts
- committing and pushing cleanup
- updating PR title, body, base, labels, reviewers, draft state, or comments
- merging a PR
- closing a superseded or duplicate PR after preservation
- proving that an already-complete cleanup requires no further mutation
Do not require a tracked-file edit when the sprint deliverable is a valid Git or GitHub mutation.
VALIDATION
Validate in the user-declared order.
Use the strongest practical checks appropriate to changed behavior.
Always review:
git diff --check
git status --short
git log --oneline --decorate -12
git branch -vv
For PR work, also inspect:
gh pr checks <number>
gh pr view <number>
gh pr diff <number>
FINAL RESPONSE
CONTEXT
- repo:
- branch/worktree:
- sprint:
- owned scope:
- forbidden scope:
- validation order honored:
OPERATIONS COMPLETED
- branches/worktrees created or reused:
- commits cherry-picked/reverted/restored:
- code/doctrine/docs changed:
- pushes:
- PR comments/labels/base/draft/review mutations:
- PRs merged:
- PRs closed:
- useful work preservation destination:
PR DISPOSITION
- PR:
- prior state:
- action:
- resulting state:
- evidence:
VALIDATION
- command:
- result:
- deferred checks run last:
- skipped checks and exact reason:
FINAL GIT / PR STATE
- git status --short:
- open PRs remaining:
- blocked PRs:
- clean base for next sprint:
NEXT COMMAND
- one exact command, or `none; cleanup complete`
A final answer without actual repo/PR mutations, preservation proof, an exact blocker, or proof that cleanup was already complete is invalid.
Clean the repo. Preserve useful work. Merge and close what is ready. Then stop.

name: Repo Sprint Executor
sprintRole: Execute any bounded repo change
useWhen: The sprint is known and must change code, doctrine, docs, branches, comments, PRs, contracts, or releases.
inspectFirst: Repo patterns, branch state, tests, validators, diff, mutation capability.
expectedOutput: Committed or remotely mutated repo progress with validation.
nextStep: Use P11 or P12.
proofGate: Commit or concrete Git/GitHub mutation evidence.
copyContent:
EXECUTE THE REPO SPRINT. DO NOT REWRITE THIS PROMPT.
Repo: xyz_repo_or_path
Sprint: xyz_sprint_name
Owned scope: xyz_owned_scope
Forbidden scope: xyz_forbidden_scope
Context or plan path: xyz_plan_directory
Your job is to change the repository, validate the change, commit it, and push or open/update a PR when the environment allows.
Do not answer with:
- a corrected prompt
- a plan only
- a handoff only
- a summary of what should be done
- a request for permission when the task is scoped and safe
- preflight notes without repo changes
If you respond by writing a better prompt instead of committing repo work, you failed.
Start with only enough preflight to avoid damaging existing work:
git status --short
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline --decorate -5
If the worktree is dirty or conflicted:
- if this sprint owns cleanup, repair it safely
- otherwise create or use an isolated worktree
- if neither is possible, stop with the exact blocker and the safest next command
If the plan path is missing, stale, blank, or placeholder, do not stall.
Inspect repo evidence: recent commits, open PRs, handoff docs, plan docs, validators, tests, scripts, artifacts, and current branch state.
Proceed from the clearest current context.
Implementation rule:
After preflight, immediately do one of these:
1. Modify tracked repo files within owned scope.
2. Prove the sprint is already complete with existing committed files and passing checks.
3. Stop with an exact blocker plus the smallest patch, diff, or file content that should be applied.
Default to option 1.
Minimum movement:
At least one useful repository mutation must occur unless the sprint is already complete or blocked. Valid movement includes tracked-file changes, commits, branch or worktree creation, cherry-picks, merges, pushes, PR comments, labels, base or draft changes, PR closure, or other owned Git/GitHub mutations.
Work rules:
- reuse existing repo patterns before inventing
- keep changes bounded
- avoid unrelated rewrites
- prefer tests, validators, scripts, docs, or manifests that enforce behavior
- when run context, artifact registry, schemas, shared output contracts, or their validators are in owned scope, implement them here
- do not turn real behavior into stubs just to pass tests
- do not commit secrets, personal data, huge logs, crash dumps, generated runtime evidence, or machine-local junk
- do not hide behind missing context when repo evidence can answer the question
- repository work may include code, doctrine, docs, comments, branches, worktrees, cherry-picks, merges, PR mutations, and releases when those actions are inside owned scope
Validation:
Run the strongest practical checks available:
1. targeted tests for changed behavior
2. relevant validators or static checks
3. build checks
4. broader checks when practical
If a check is skipped, say why and name the exact command that should be run later.
Commit and push contract:
git diff --check
git status --short
git diff --stat
git diff
git add <changed tracked files>
git commit -m "<useful message>"
git push -u origin <branch>
Open or update a PR when appropriate.
Final response must not include a next-agent prompt.
Final response format:
CONTEXT
- repo:
- branch:
- sprint:
- scope:
- forbidden scope:
WORK COMMITTED
- summary:
- files changed:
- commit SHA:
- pushed: yes/no
- PR: URL or not opened
VALIDATION
- command:
- result:
- skipped checks:
BLOCKERS / GAPS
- blocker:
- gap:
- risk:
FINAL GIT STATE
- git status --short:
NEXT COMMAND
- one exact command:
A final response without a commit SHA, concrete Git/GitHub mutation evidence, patch/diff, exact blocker, or proof that the work was already complete is invalid.
Do the repo work. Commit it. Then stop.

name: Live Runtime Proof Executor

inspectFirst: Safety docs, launchers, sessions, runtime logs, proof chain.

nextStep: Use P12.
proofGate: Observed behavior is not confused with ACK/static proof.
copyContent:
You are executing a live/runtime proof sprint.
Repo:
`xyz_repo_or_path`
Sprint:
`xyz_runtime_sprint`
Lane:
runtime proof / live behavior evidence
Scope:
prove the specific app/game/browser/service behavior under controlled conditions
Forbidden scope:
unrelated feature work, personal data mutation, save/account mutation, free resources, broad refactors, lower-proof success claims
Dependency:
Do not start until repo floor/hygiene is known safe and required launcher/focus/session safety is in place. If dependency is skipped, state why.
Before runtime:
- stop the app/game/service first if required by repo doctrine
- use repo-owned launcher/session scripts
- do not rely on terminal focus
- bound every wait
- use disposable/safe state only
- do not mutate personal saves/accounts/data
Required proof chain:
- stop/safe-start completed if required
- launcher/session attach succeeded
- target surface ready
- command or trigger issued
- command ACK observed if applicable
- behavior actually observed
- runtime artifact/log collected
- failure reason captured if behavior did not occur
Proof rule:
Do not claim live runtime proof from static tests, contract checks, launcher proof, command ACK, or route/request issuance alone. Name the exact proof level reached.

- targeted build/test before runtime
- runtime proof collection
- log/artifact grep proving the required chain
- git diff --check
- git status --short

copyContent:
You are adding local hook and artifact hygiene.
Repo:
`xyz_repo_or_path`
Sprint:
Local hook and artifact hygiene
Lane:
repo hygiene / safety rails

prevent generated evidence, logs, saves, local tool installs, crash dumps, secrets, and machine-local junk from leaking into commits
Forbidden scope:
product behavior changes, runtime execution, mandatory global hooks, broad blockers that slow normal code/docs work

- .githooks if present

- artifact/output conventions

- hook output tells the operator what to do next

- no broad blocking of normal docs/code changes

copyContent:

Consumer repo or repos: xyz_consumer_repos
Sprint: xyz_sprint_name

CONTRIBUTION BOUNDARY
